html:
Theodor Svedberg, known as The Svedberg (pronunciation missing), was born on August 30, 1884, in Fleräng, Valbo parish, Gävleborg County, and died on February 26, 1971, in Kopparberg, Örebro County. He was a Swedish chemist, professor, painter, and illustrator.

He was a professor of physical chemistry at Uppsala University from 1912 to 1949, director of the Gustaf Werner Institute for Nuclear Chemistry in Uppsala from 1949 to 1967, and received the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1926 for his studies of dispersed systems using his analytical ultracentrifuge.

== Biography == Svedberg was the son of mill manager Elias Svedberg and Märta Augusta Maria Alstermark. He spent parts of his childhood at Älvestorps Manor in Västmanland, married four times, and had a total of twelve children. His first wife was Dr. Andrea Andreen (1909–1915), with whom he had two children: Elias Svedberg and Hillevi Svedberg. In his second marriage (1916–1938), he married Jane Charlotte Frodi, who held a Master's degree in philosophy. His third marriage was to Ingrid Anna Karolina Blomquist (1938–1947), and his fourth wife, from 1948 until her death, was Karin Margit Hallén (1920-2019), also holding a Master's degree in philosophy. In the second, third, and fourth marriages, he fathered five sons and five daughters. A granddaughter of Svedberg is the author Karolina Ramqvist.

After attending schools in Köping, Örebro, and Gothenburg, where he obtained his high school diploma in December 1903, Svedberg enrolled at Uppsala University in January 1904. He earned a Bachelor's degree in philosophy in 1905, a Master's degree in 1907, and a Doctoral degree in 1908 with his dissertation "Studien zur Lehre von den kolloiden Lösungen" ("Studies on the Theory of Colloidal Solutions"). He served as an assistant professor in chemistry at Uppsala from 1905 to 1909 and was appointed acting docent in 1907. From 1909, he held a special teaching position in physical chemistry, and in 1912, he was appointed Professor of this subject.

Svedberg's work on colloids supported the theories of Brownian motion proposed by Albert Einstein and the Polish geophysicist Marian Smoluchowski. During his research, he also developed analytical ultracentrifugation and demonstrated its usefulness for separating purified proteins. The unit "Svedberg," denoted by the symbol S, is named after him. It is a measure of sedimentation rate, equivalent to 10-13 seconds or 100 femtoseconds.

Svedberg was Professor of Physical Chemistry at Uppsala University from 1912 to 1949 and subsequently Director of the Gustaf Werner Institute (GWI) for Nuclear Chemistry in Uppsala. As a hobby, he engaged in artistic pursuits from his youth, painting scenes from places in Sweden and abroad. He participated in the art exhibition of the student newspaper "Ergo" at Uppsala University in 1925, and in 1948, he exhibited several paintings with South American themes at the Uppsala student exhibition. At the "Signerad textil" exhibition held at Nordiska Kompaniet and Röhsska Museum of Decorative Arts, he displayed two printed fabrics inspired by his work in atomic and chromosome research. His tempera painting depicting a scene from Uppsala was republished in Allhems's "A Pictorial Work about Uppsala" in 1954. His artwork consists of landscape depictions created using oil paints, tempera, or watercolors.

In the later years of his life, Svedberg lived in Kopparberg, where he is also buried.

== Honorary Titles ==

=== Awards === Svedberg was elected a member of the Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences in 1913, of the Royal Scientific Society in Uppsala in 1921, and of the Royal Physiographic Society in Lund in 1922. He was appointed honorary doctor of technology at the Royal Institute of Technology in 1944.

  • Nobel Prize in Chemistry, 1926
  • Björkénska Prize, 1926
  • Foreign member of the Royal Society, June 15, 1944
  • Franklin Medal, 1949
  • Honorary doctorate from the Sorbonne, December 2, 1950

[Edit Wikidata]

=== Naming === The Svedberg Prize is named after him. The particle accelerator facility, The Svedberg Laboratory (formerly the Gustaf Werner Institute), in Uppsala was founded by and named after Svedberg. A lunar crater is named after him. It was named Svedberg in 2009 by the International Astronomical Union.

== Bibliography ==

  • Studien zur Lehre von den kolloiden Lösungen (1907), doctoral dissertation, Libris 1841295
  • Die Existenz der Moleküle (1912)
  • Materien (1912)
  • Arbetets dekadens (1915)
  • Forskning och industri (1918)
  • Strindberg som kemist (1918)
  • The Ultracentrifuge (1940)
  • Fragment. Selection and introduction by Carl Frängsmyr, Acta universitatis upsaliensis, series B. Invitations, 169 (2012).

== Sources == Svenskt konstnärslexikon part V, pages 336-337, Allhems förlag, Malmö. Libris 8390293

How certain are we about this?

People from the 20th century are extremely well-documented. Uncertainty rarely concerns facts but rather interpretation and evaluation, which can change over time and depending on the observer.
tldr: Theodor Svedberg was a Swedish chemist who won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1926 for his research on the movement and separation of small particles.
lattlast:
Theodor Svedberg was born in 1884 and worked for many years as a professor at Uppsala University. He became famous for developing a machine called the ultracentrifuge. This machine allowed researchers to separate proteins from each other, which is important in chemistry and medicine.

Svedberg received the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1926 for his work on these small particles called colloids. His contributions were so significant that a unit of measurement used in chemistry bears his name, as do a laboratory in Uppsala and a crater on the Moon.
